frameworks.name: cortical inhibition-excitation balance
frameworks.one_line: tDCS presumed to shift occipital E/I balance probed via orientation discrimination

frameworks.name: placebo effect
frameworks.one_line: performance gains attributed to expectation rather than active cortical modulation

core_question: Does occipital tDCS modulate visual orientation discrimination performance, or are observed gains attributable to placebo?
key_assumptions: orientation discrimination performance reflects occipital inhibition-excitation balance
prior work showed no practice effects across two consecutive ODT runs
sham tDCS isolates expectation effects from active stimulation
manipulations: tDCS polarity: sham vs anodal vs cathodal occipital stimulation
delay between ODT runs: 2 min vs 10 min with no stimulation
key_findings: performance improved on the second ODT run irrespective of tDCS polarity
only sham-tDCS, not extended delay alone, produced improved second-run performance
interpretations: the observed gains reflect a tDCS placebo effect rather than active neuromodulation
tDCS can elicit performance changes even without active cortical modulation
